deeper_dives.title: Natural Language → 3D Scenes: The Pipeline That Didn't Exist 6 Months Ago
deeper_dives.thesis: MCP + Blender + Tripo + Claude Code together create a natural language 3D scene builder — describe what you want, get a full 3D scene. This was impossible before MCP existed.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Six months ago, creating a 3D scene meant hours in Blender learning hotkeys, topology, and lighting. Now you type 'a cozy cabin in snow with warm interior lighting' and MCP bridges Claude Code to Blender, Tripo generates the meshes, and Hunyuan3D handles the geometry. The Model Context Protocol is the glue — it lets Claude manipulate Blender like a human operator, but faster. This isn't just automation; it's a new creative medium where language IS the modeling tool. The barrier to 3D went from years of training to a well-written sentence.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Set up Blender MCP server → connect to Claude Code → use Tripo/Hunyuan3D for mesh gen → describe scenes in plain English → get rigged, lit 3D environments. Add TouchDesigner for live interaction.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Blender (free), Claude Code subscription, Tripo API key (free tier available), MCP server setup (~15 min)

deeper_dives.title: The Autonomous Brand Machine: Claude Code as Your Entire Marketing Team
deeper_dives.thesis: Claude Code + Playwright MCP + Higgsfield + knowledge graphs = a system that generates ads, UGC, 3D content, and posts it — autonomously. One person runs what used to need a 5-person team.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: The traditional marketing team — copywriter, designer, videographer, social media manager, strategist — costs $30K+/month. This pipeline replaces all five roles with one Claude Code session that runs autonomously. Playwright MCP handles posting, Higgsfield generates motion content, the Obsidian knowledge graph ensures brand consistency across everything generated. The key insight: auto-approve mode means Claude doesn't stop to ask permission. You set the strategy, the machine executes 24/7. One person with this stack produces more content than a 5-person team, and it gets smarter each cycle because CLAUDE.md captures what works.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Build Obsidian knowledge base of brand assets → connect via Graphify → Claude Code generates ads/UGC via Higgsfield → Playwright MCP posts to social → iOS Shortcuts for mobile triggers. Total cost: Claude subscription.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Claude Code, Playwright MCP, Higgsfield account, Obsidian vault, social media accounts with API access

deeper_dives.title: Physics Models → Market Alpha: When Scientists Become Traders
deeper_dives.thesis: 26 posts map physics/math models to trading — avalanche theory, Ising models, phase space reconstruction. The meta-insight: the quant edge isn't in finance, it's in importing models from other fields.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Wall Street's edge has always come from importing ideas other traders haven't seen. Renaissance Technologies hired physicists, not MBAs. This catalog maps the same playbook: Ising models from magnetism predict herd behavior, Omori's earthquake law models post-crash dynamics, phase space reconstruction from topology reveals hidden BTC patterns. The meta-insight isn't any single model — it's that the best alpha comes from fields nobody on Wall Street is reading. Each physics paper is a potential trading signal that quant Twitter hasn't priced in yet. The 26 posts here form a complete curriculum for building a cross-disciplinary signal ensemble.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Study the cross-disciplinary models. Build a signal ensemble that combines 3+ physics-derived indicators. Backtest with Monte Carlo validation. Deploy as an AI agent that runs daily.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Python + NumPy/SciPy, basic statistics, willingness to read physics papers, backtesting framework (backtrader or custom)

entries.title: Phase Space Reconstruction — unfolding hidden BTC volatility dynamics
entries.summary: Uses Phase Space Reconstruction (PSR) with time delay tau=3 to unfold BTCUSD volatility into a multi-dimensional manifold. Reveals whether market is in stable regime or chaotic transition. Key for quantitative regime detection.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

entries.title: Avalanche Model — self-organized criticality explains market crashes
entries.summary: Markets reach self-organized criticality where small perturbations trigger cascading failures. The 'news' doesn't cause the crash — the system was already at a critical state. Applies sandpile/avalanche dynamics from physics to explain market fragility.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

entries.title: Ising Model — modeling herd behavior and market phase transitions
entries.summary: 3D lattice visualization of the Ising model applied to financial markets. Blue=sellers, orange=buyers. At high volatility the market is random; as social pressure increases, clusters form leading to spontaneous symmetry breaking (crashes/trends). Built in Python for agent-based modeling.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

entries.title: Heston Model — stochastic volatility for realistic options pricing
entries.summary: Explains why Black-Scholes fails (constant vol assumption) and how the Heston Model introduces stochastic volatility. Captures the volatility smile and skew that basic models miss. Essential for options pricing.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

entries.title: Omori's Law — earthquake aftershock math applied to market crashes
entries.summary: Applies geophysics' Omori's Law to financial markets. Post-crash volatility aftershocks decay at a predictable rate following power-law dynamics, not randomly. Shows March 2020 crash decay pattern. DM-bait for code repo.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

entries.title: Wasserstein Distance — measuring the backtest-to-live reality gap
entries.summary: Uses Wasserstein Distance and Sinkhorn Divergence to mathematically measure drift between backtest distributions (blue) and live market conditions (orange). Detects distribution shift that kills more strategies than bad fees.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

deeper_dives.title: The Self-Improving AI Workspace: Claude Code That Gets Smarter Every Session
deeper_dives.thesis: CLAUDE.md + primer.md + memory hooks + Obsidian + parallel worktrees = an AI dev environment that learns your preferences, remembers context, and runs multiple streams simultaneously.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Most people use Claude as a chatbot — ask a question, get an answer, context dies. This stack makes Claude a persistent collaborator that remembers your preferences, coding style, and project history across sessions. CLAUDE.md is the living rulebook (it self-improves when you correct mistakes), primer.md carries session state across context resets, parallel worktrees let you run 10-15 Claude sessions simultaneously on different parts of the same codebase. The compounding effect is real: after a week, Claude knows your patterns better than a new hire would after a month. After a month, it's like having a senior engineer who's read every line of your code.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Set up CLAUDE.md with self-improvement rules → add primer.md for session continuity → connect Obsidian vault → enable parallel worktrees for concurrent work → watch the system compound over weeks.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Claude Code subscription, git (for worktrees), Obsidian (optional but recommended), ~30 min initial setup

deeper_dives.title: Napkin Sketch → Production Asset: The 5-Tool Creative Pipeline
deeper_dives.thesis: A hand-drawn sketch can become a production-ready 3D animation in under an hour by chaining Procreate → Magnific AI → 3DAI Studio → Move AI → final render. Each tool handles one transformation step.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: The gap between 'I have an idea' and 'I have a production asset' used to be weeks of specialized work across multiple disciplines. This pipeline collapses it to under an hour by chaining five AI tools, each handling one transformation: sketch → upscale → 3D mesh → motion → final render. The key is that each tool is best-in-class at its specific step, and they hand off cleanly. A napkin doodle at lunch becomes a rigged, animated 3D character by dinner. For indie creators, this is the difference between having ideas and shipping them.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Draw in Procreate → upscale with Magnific → generate 3D mesh with Hunyuan3D or 3DAI → capture motion with Move AI → composite with Kling or Runway. Total time: ~45 minutes per asset.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Procreate or any drawing app, Magnific AI, Hunyuan3D-2 or 3DAI Studio, Kling or Runway account

deeper_dives.title: Body → Machine → Art: Real-Time Interactive Installations Anyone Can Build
deeper_dives.thesis: Kinect/MediaPipe body tracking + TouchDesigner + Unreal Engine = interactive art installations that respond to human movement. Previously needed a studio budget; now runs on a gaming laptop.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Interactive art installations used to require a $50K+ budget: custom hardware, specialized software, a team of creative technologists. Now MediaPipe runs body tracking on a $30 webcam, TouchDesigner handles the visual pipeline, and a $200 projector fills a wall. The total cost dropped 99%. This democratization means independent artists can build gallery-quality interactive work in their apartment. The posts here map the complete stack from body input to visual output, including real examples of installations that respond to movement, sound, and light.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: MediaPipe for body tracking (free, runs on webcam) → pipe data to TouchDesigner → drive visuals in Unreal or project with a $200 projector. Gallery-quality interactive art for under $500 in hardware.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Webcam, TouchDesigner (free for non-commercial), projector ($200+), laptop with decent GPU

deeper_dives.title: The One-Person Video Studio: AI Tools That Replace a Production Team
deeper_dives.thesis: Runway + Kling + rotoscoping + motion brush = professional video production without a crew, studio, or actors. Direct scenes with text prompts and motion controls.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Professional video production has three bottlenecks: talent (actors/crew), equipment (cameras/lighting/studio), and post-production (editing/VFX). AI tools collapse all three. Runway's node canvas replaces the editing suite. Kling's motion brush replaces the director ('move this character's arm here'). Rotoscoping lets you insert yourself into any scene without a green screen. OpenScreen handles screen recording for tutorials. The result: one person with a laptop produces content that previously needed a production team. The cost dropped from $10K/video to $30/month in subscriptions.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Script with Claude → generate base footage with Runway/Kling → direct characters with motion brush → rotoscope yourself in → edit on the node canvas → export. Cost: ~$30/month in AI tools.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Runway account (~$12/mo), Kling (free tier), OpenScreen (free), basic video editing concept

deeper_dives.title: Code as Canvas: When Algorithms Become the Artist
deeper_dives.thesis: Ant colonies, boid simulations, particle systems, and generative algorithms create art that's alive — emergent beauty from simple rules. The artist writes the rules, not the pixels.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Traditional art is about control — every brushstroke is intentional. Generative art inverts this: you write rules, and beauty emerges from complexity. Ant colony simulations create organic trail patterns. Boid flocking algorithms produce mesmerizing crowd dynamics. Particle systems generate physics-driven visual poetry. The artist's role shifts from pixel-pusher to system designer. The posts here show the full spectrum: from mathematical simulations to creative coding gardens to accidental beauty from music+code. This is art that's alive — it never produces the same output twice.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Pick a natural system (flocking, diffusion, growth). Code it in p5.js or TouchDesigner. Add interaction (sound, body tracking, data feeds). The art emerges — you don't draw it, you evolve it.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: p5.js or Processing (both free), basic programming concepts, optional: TouchDesigner for real-time interaction

entries.title: Ant Colony Optimization sim — pheromone feedback + diffusion PDE
entries.summary: @simulateitnow explains ACO / stigmergy with the diffusion-evaporation equation ∂τ/∂t = Δτ + D∇²τ - ρτ, showing how short paths win through faster pheromone reinforcement. Great visual reference for swarm optimization.

entries.title: Shepherd dog flock simulation — pressure-field + boids rules
entries.summary: @simulateitnow explains a flock-herding simulation combining a pressure-field force F=(1-d/R)^1.2 with classic boids (cohesion/alignment/separation). Great reference for swarm/multi-agent simulation math.

deeper_dives.title: Why Your Agent Sucks: Architecture Patterns That Actually Work
deeper_dives.thesis: Better models won't fix broken agent architecture. The gap between demo agents and production agents is context management, memory hierarchy, and harness engineering — not model capability.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Everyone's building agents. Almost nobody's building agents that work past the demo. The 103 new entries from this session surfaced a pattern: the posts that actually teach agent building focus on architecture — context management, memory hierarchies, harness engineering — not on which model to use. The context vs memory vs harness framework is the clearest mental model we've seen for why most agents fail: they dump everything into context, have no persistent memory, and lack structural guardrails. This dive connects the theory (architecture patterns) with the infrastructure (Onyx, MCP, Kimi) needed to implement it.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Audit your agent's context window usage → separate context (ephemeral) from memory (persistent) from harness (structural) → implement retrieval layer (Onyx or similar) → add tool-use via MCP → test with Claude Code as the orchestrator → measure task completion, not conversation quality.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Familiarity with LLM APIs, basic understanding of RAG. Claude Code or similar agent framework.

deeper_dives.title: 25 Agents Walk Into a Market: AI-Powered Trading Infrastructure
deeper_dives.thesis: Quantitative finance is being rebuilt on AI agents — not as black-box predictors, but as multi-agent debate systems that combine physics models, regime detection, and autonomous execution.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: The stock2 collection revealed something the quant collection hinted at: the next generation of trading systems isn't a single model predicting price direction. It's a parliament of specialized agents — one running HMMs for regime detection, one computing Heston vol surfaces, one monitoring Cox process bursts — that debate and vote before any trade executes. The 25-agent debate system entry is the architecture pattern; the quant physics models are the individual specialist brains. Together they form a trading infrastructure where agents with different world-models challenge each other's assumptions in real time.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Build regime detection with HMMs → layer Heston vol surface for options → add phase space reconstruction for hidden dynamics → implement A-S market making for execution → wrap in multi-agent debate system where agents specialize by model type → score on real P&L, not backtest metrics.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Python, basic stochastic calculus concepts, market data API access. Familiarity with at least one quant model.

entries.title: Hidden Markov Models — detecting market regime shifts automatically
entries.summary: Uses HMMs to mathematically deduce hidden market states from observable returns and volatility. Identifies low-vol bull, high-vol bear, and recovery regimes. Better than lagging indicators for strategy selection.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

entries.title: Heston Model — stochastic volatility for realistic options pricing
entries.summary: Explains why Black-Scholes fails (constant vol assumption) and how the Heston Model introduces stochastic volatility. Captures the volatility smile and skew that basic models miss. Essential for options pricing.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

entries.title: Phase Space Reconstruction — unfolding hidden BTC volatility dynamics
entries.summary: Uses Phase Space Reconstruction (PSR) with time delay tau=3 to unfold BTCUSD volatility into a multi-dimensional manifold. Reveals whether market is in stable regime or chaotic transition. Key for quantitative regime detection.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

entries.title: Avellaneda-Stoikov — optimal spread calculation for market makers
entries.summary: The Avellaneda-Stoikov model solves the stochastic control problem for market making. Calculates optimal bid-ask spread based on volatility, time horizon, and inventory. Foundation of modern HFT market making. DM-bait for code repo.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

entries.title: Cox Process — doubly stochastic Poisson for bursty market events
entries.summary: Standard Poisson processes assume constant event rates, but markets are bursty. The Cox Process (Doubly Stochastic Poisson) uses a CIR-driven random intensity to model trade arrivals, defaults, and volatility spikes more realistically.
entries.creator: quant.traderr

deeper_dives.title: The Claude Code Power User's Toolkit: From Token Waste to Terminal Dominance
deeper_dives.thesis: Claude Code's ceiling isn't the model — it's your configuration. CLAUDE.md, primer.md, hooks, parallel worktrees, and memory systems turn a chatbot into an autonomous engineering team.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: Most Claude Code users treat it as a fancy autocomplete. The Boris Cherny posts revealed the real architecture: CLAUDE.md as persistent instruction set, primer.md as session memory, hooks as automation triggers, and parallel worktrees for concurrent agents. Combined with the 'burning tokens is a skill issue' insight from ai6, this forms a complete toolkit for going from 'talking to a chatbot' to 'managing an autonomous engineering team.' The gap between casual and power user is entirely configuration — no new tools needed, just better use of what's already there.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Write a CLAUDE.md with project rules + persona → add primer.md for session state → install hooks for automation → set up parallel worktrees for multi-agent work → install the 7 key repos → configure memory system → measure token usage and optimize.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Claude Code subscription, git (for worktrees), 30 minutes to configure CLAUDE.md and primer.md

entries.title: Boris Cherny's Claude Code workflow — parallel worktrees + plan mode

deeper_dives.title: The One-Person Creative Empire: AI Tools That Replace an Entire Agency
deeper_dives.thesis: Claude Code + Figma + Runway + Higgsfield + TouchDesigner enables a single person to produce marketing, video, interactive art, and web design at agency quality — the creative middle class is being born.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: The creative collection tells a single story: the minimum viable creative agency is now one person with the right AI tools. Not in a 'kinda works if you squint' way — in a 'produced a full marketing campaign, interactive art installation, and production website in a weekend' way. Claude Code isn't just a coding tool here; it's the orchestration layer that connects Figma (design), Runway/Higgsfield (video), TouchDesigner (interactive), and React (web) into a unified creative pipeline. The creative middle class — people with taste but not a team — now has a studio.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Set up Claude Code as creative orchestrator → connect Figma for design → add Runway/Higgsfield for video → TouchDesigner + MediaPipe for interactive → Cursor for web builds → run marketing, design, video, and interactive art from a single workstation.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Claude Code, Figma (free tier), TouchDesigner (free), basic comfort with terminal. Budget for Runway/Higgsfield optional.

deeper_dives.title: Prompt Engineering Isn't Dead — It Just Grew Up
deeper_dives.thesis: Prompt engineering evolved from 'add please' to a real discipline: structured frameworks, adversarial testing, llms.txt standards, and system-level prompt architecture that shapes model behavior at scale.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: The 'prompt engineering is dead' crowd missed the plot. It didn't die — it professionalized. Anthropic's own leaked system prompt shows structured instruction architecture. llms.txt is becoming a web standard. Google published a formal framework. The L1B3RT4S jailbreaks prove that prompt security is a real engineering discipline. And GitReverse shows you can reverse-engineer any output back to its prompt. This isn't 'ask nicely' anymore — it's systems engineering for language models.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Study Anthropic's leaked system prompt for structure patterns → implement llms.txt for your own sites → build a prompt library organized by use case (research, code, creative) → test prompts adversarially with L1B3RT4S techniques → add GitReverse to your workflow for learning from others' prompts.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: Any LLM API access. Basic understanding of system vs user prompts.

deeper_dives.title: The Incentive Trap: Game Theory for the AI Age
deeper_dives.thesis: Game theory isn't abstract math — it's the operating system behind every AI product decision, every market move, and every platform play. The entries in this collection reveal the incentive structures hiding in plain sight.
deeper_dives.why_it_matters: The game-theory collection is new and small but punches above its weight. Elon's $1 is a masterclass in incentive design — the trap is always in the commitment structure, not the headline number. Combined with the AI origin story (decades of academic game theory producing today's landscape) and the physics-as-strategy entries, this collection reveals that every major decision has a game-theoretic structure. Understanding it is the difference between being a player and being played.
deeper_dives.action_sketch: Map the incentive structure before making any decision → identify who benefits from each outcome → look for lock-in mechanisms (Elon's $1 pattern) → study failed strategies to understand what the 'game' actually is → apply physics intuition (equilibria, forces) to strategic situations.
deeper_dives.prerequisites: None — these are mental models, not technical implementations.
